title: The first-cut wall
summary: A Beirut barber keeps a photograph of every customer's first haircut.
body:
Khalil has been a barber in Beirut for thirty-eight years. His shop is small. It has one chair, one mirror, one fan, and one rule. Every new child gets a photograph after their first haircut.

The wall behind the chair is covered with these photographs. Some are old and yellow. Some are new. The oldest photograph is from 1987. The boy in it is now a doctor in Paris.

Khalil does not put the photographs in any order. He says, 'Time in this shop is a mess. That is good. A child of 1991 is next to a child of 2022. They are both children to me.'

Last week, a young father came in with his small son. The son was three years old and afraid of the scissors. He cried. The father said, 'I cried too, here, in this chair. I was three. My father brought me.'

Khalil smiled. He stood up and looked at the wall. After a minute, he pointed at a small photograph high on the wall. 'This is you. 1995. You wore a red sweater.'

The father did not believe him. He stood up. He looked closely. It was him. He sat down again, quiet.

The son stopped crying. He watched his father, then he watched Khalil. He let Khalil cut his hair. After, Khalil took a new photograph and put it next to the old one.

Two small faces, twenty-eight years apart, side by side on a quiet wall.

annotations.meaningVi: vàng (ở đây: ảnh cũ ngả vàng)
theo thứ tự nào — 'not in any order' = không sắp theo thứ tự
cũ và ngả vàng (nói về ảnh cũ)

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect for duration: 'has been … for'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: A2 dùng present perfect ('has/have + V3') với 'for + khoảng thời gian' để diễn tả việc bắt đầu trong quá khứ và còn tiếp diễn đến hiện tại. Trong bài: 'Khalil has been a barber in Beirut for thirty-eight years.' Ông bắt đầu làm thợ cắt tóc cách đây 38 năm và VẪN đang làm. So sánh với 'was' (quá khứ đơn) chỉ diễn tả việc đã kết thúc. Cấu trúc: chủ ngữ + has/have + V3 + for + khoảng thời gian.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: A2 uses the present perfect ('has/have + past participle') with 'for + duration' for actions that started in the past and continue now. From the passage: 'Khalil has been a barber in Beirut for thirty-eight years.' He began thirty-eight years ago and is STILL a barber. Compare with 'was' (past simple), which signals a finished situation. Structure: subject + has/have + past participle + for + duration.

translationVi:
Khalil đã làm thợ cắt tóc ở Beirut ba mươi tám năm. Cửa hàng ông nhỏ. Có một ghế, một gương, một quạt và một quy tắc. Mỗi đứa trẻ mới đến đều được tặng một tấm ảnh sau lần cắt tóc đầu tiên.

Bức tường sau ghế phủ kín những tấm ảnh ấy. Có ảnh cũ và ngả vàng. Có ảnh mới và sáng bóng. Ảnh cũ nhất từ năm 1987. Cậu bé trong ảnh nay là bác sĩ ở Paris. Anh ấy gửi cho Khalil một tấm bưu thiếp năm ngoái.

Khalil không xếp ảnh theo thứ tự nào. Ông nói: 'Thời gian trong cửa hàng này lộn xộn. Vậy là tốt. Một đứa trẻ năm 1991 nằm cạnh một đứa trẻ năm 2022. Với tôi cả hai đều là trẻ con.'

Tuần trước, một người cha trẻ vào với cậu con trai nhỏ. Cậu bé ba tuổi và sợ kéo. Cậu khóc. Người cha nói: 'Tôi cũng khóc, ở đây, trên chiếc ghế này. Tôi ba tuổi. Cha tôi dẫn tôi đến.'

Khalil mỉm cười. Ông đứng dậy nhìn vào tường. Sau một lát, ông chỉ vào một tấm ảnh nhỏ cao trên tường. 'Đây là anh. Năm 1995. Anh mặc áo len đỏ.'

Người cha không tin. Anh đứng dậy. Anh nhìn kỹ. Đúng là anh. Anh ngồi xuống lại, lặng yên.

Cậu bé thôi khóc. Cậu nhìn cha, rồi nhìn Khalil. Cậu để Khalil cắt tóc. Cắt xong, Khalil chụp một tấm ảnh mới và đặt nó cạnh tấm cũ.

Hai gương mặt nhỏ, cách nhau hai mươi tám năm, kề bên nhau trên một bức tường yên lặng.

questions.prompt: How long has Khalil been a barber?
What does every new child get after their first haircut?
What is on the wall behind the chair?
options: Photographs of children after their first haircut.
questions.explanation: 'The wall behind the chair is covered with these photographs.'

questions.prompt: From what year is the oldest photograph?
Where is the boy in the oldest photograph now?
questions.explanation: 'The boy in it is now a doctor in Paris.'

questions.prompt: Why does Khalil not put the photos in order?
options: He says time in the shop is a mess, and to him every child is just a child.

questions.prompt: What happened in 1995, according to Khalil?
options: He cut the hair of the man who came in last week, when the man was three.
questions.explanation: 'This is you. 1995. You wore a red sweater.'

questions.prompt: What did the young father wear in his first-haircut photograph?
What does the son do after his father is quiet?
options: Stops crying, watches his father and Khalil, and lets Khalil cut his hair.
questions.explanation: Direct from paragraph after father sits.

questions.prompt: What does Khalil do with the new photograph at the end?
options: Puts it next to the father's old one on the wall.
Gives it to the father to keep.
questions.explanation: 'Took a new photograph and put it next to the old one.'

options: A barber's wall of first-haircut photographs collapses time, showing father and son as the same age in a single look.
Lebanese children are afraid of scissors.
questions.explanation: Closing image of the two photos side by side.

cloze.template:
Khalil has been a {{b1}} in Beirut for thirty-eight years. His shop is small. It has one chair, one {{b2}}, one fan, and one rule. Every new child gets a photograph after their first {{b3}}.

The wall behind the chair is covered with these photographs. Some are old and yellow. Some are new. The oldest photograph is from {{b4}}. The boy in it is now a doctor in {{b5}}.

Khalil does not put the photographs in any {{b6}}. He says, 'Time in this shop is a mess. That is good. A child of 1991 is next to a child of 2022. They are both children to me.'

Last week, a young father came in with his small son. The son was three years old and {{b7}} of the scissors. He cried. The father said, 'I cried too, here, in this chair. I was three. My father brought me.'

Khalil smiled. He stood up and looked at the wall. After a minute, he pointed at a small photograph high on the wall. 'This is you. 1995. You wore a {{b8}} sweater.'

The father did not believe him. He stood up. He looked closely. It was him. He sat down again, quiet.

The son stopped crying. He watched his father, then he watched Khalil. He let Khalil cut his hair. After, Khalil took a new photograph and put it next to the old one.

Two small faces, twenty-eight {{b9}} apart, side by {{b10}} on a quiet wall.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Khalil refuses to organise the photographs by date. What does he gain by putting a 1991 child next to a 2022 child — and what is lost if we always insist on chronological order in how we remember people?
